subject: mathematics

sections.title: Tally Marks on Bones and Other Surfaces
Number Names Obtained by Counting in Twos
Variations on the Egyptian System and the Notion of Base

prose_summary:
Section 3.1 identifies the historical context for the chapter by tracing the origins of counting to the Stone Age: humans needed to track food, animals, trade goods, and celestial events. It introduces the origin of the modern number system in ancient India, noting that the Yajurveda Samhita named numbers as powers of ten, that the Bakhshali manuscript (c. 3rd century CE) contains the earliest known use of ten digits including zero, and that Aryabhata (499 CE) systematised the Indian system. The chapter then traces the transmission of Hindu numerals through the Arab world (Al-Khwarizmi, Al-Kindi, c. 825–830 CE) to Europe via Fibonacci (c. 1200 CE), ultimately displacing Roman numerals by the 17th century.

Section 3.2.I recalls the use of body parts as counting tools, illustrated by a group from Papua New Guinea who use 27 body-part locations as a standard counting sequence, showing that physical objects can serve as a number system.

Section 3.2.II recalls tally marks on bones as one of the oldest known forms of number representation, describing the Lebombo bone (~44,000 years old, 29 notches) and the Ishango bone (20,000–35,000 years old) as archaeological evidence that notch-counting was a widespread early practice.

Section 3.2.III identifies and then reasons about the Gumulgal (Australia), Bakairi (South America), and Bushmen (South Africa) number systems, all of which count in twos using compound names (e.g., ukasar-ukasar-urapon for 5). The section reasons that counting in groups — here groups of two — is more efficient than a pure tally, and introduces the general idea that a group size (2, 5, 10, or 20) can serve as the organising unit of a number system. It connects this to the human perceptual limit of immediately recognising collections of fewer than five objects.

Section 3.2.IV applies Roman numeral conventions by introducing landmark numbers (I, V, X, L, C, D, M) and demonstrating how any number is grouped into these landmarks from largest to smallest. The section shows that grouping by a sequence of different landmark numbers (rather than a single group size) is more efficient for large-number representation, but identifies that Roman arithmetic — particularly multiplication and division — is cumbersome because the landmark numbers are not powers of a fixed base.

Section 3.3.I applies the Egyptian number system, in which landmark numbers are successive powers of 10 (1, 10, 100, … 10^7) each assigned a unique hieroglyphic symbol. Numbers are represented by grouping into these landmark numbers and concatenating the symbols. The section demonstrates encoding (e.g., 324 = 100+100+100+10+10+4) and connects the Egyptian approach to the idea of a base.

Section 3.3.II reasons through and then applies the generalisation of the Egyptian idea: any fixed integer n ≥ 2 can serve as a base, with landmark numbers being the powers of n. The section constructs a base-5 system with its own symbols, represents numbers in it (e.g., 143 = 125+5+5+5+1+1+1), and formalises the definition of a base-n number system. It then applies these systems to carry out addition and multiplication in both Egyptian and base-5 notations, showing that the base structure makes arithmetic systematic: multiplying any landmark number by the base yields the next landmark number, and the distributive law operates cleanly across landmark-number decompositions. The decimal abacus (11th century, lines representing powers of 10, counters on lines) is described as a calculation device that leveraged the base-10 structure.

Section 3.3.III reasons about the fundamental shortcoming of the Egyptian system: as numbers grow arbitrarily large, new symbols are needed for each new power of ten, so the system cannot represent all numbers with a finite symbol set. This sets up the need for a positional (place value) system.

Section 3.4.I introduces the Mesopotamian sexagesimal (base-60) system and then reasons through the key innovation of place value. Numbers 1–59 are represented using two symbols (for 1 and for 10), and each position in a numeral corresponds to a successive power of 60. Dropping the landmark-number labels and relying on position alone allows any number to be written with a finite symbol set. The section defines a positional number system (place value system) and notes the residual defect: blank spaces to represent skipped powers of 60 created ambiguity. The later Mesopotamian solution — a placeholder symbol (analogous to 0) used in medial positions — is introduced but its limitation (not used at the end of numerals) is also noted.

Section 3.4.II recalls the Mayan civilisation's independently developed place value system (3rd–10th centuries CE), which used a dot for 1, a bar for 5, and a seashell symbol as a zero placeholder. The Mayan system is almost base-20 but with landmark numbers 1, 20, 360, 7200 (not 400 = 20²) due to calendar influences. Despite lacking a true base structure, its place value notation and zero placeholder are recognised as important advances.

Section 3.4.III recalls the Chinese rod numeral system (decimal, base-10), in which Zong (vertical) and Heng (horizontal) rod symbols alternate by position to distinguish adjacent place values. Like Mesopotamian notation, blank spaces indicated absent powers; the more uniform symbol sizes made blank spaces easier to identify. The Chinese system with a zero symbol would constitute a fully developed place value system.

Section 3.4.IV reasons about why the Hindu number system is the culmination of all the ideas surveyed. It is a base-10 place value system with 10 distinct digit symbols (0–9), where 0 functions not merely as a placeholder but as a number in its own right on which arithmetic operations are defined. Aryabhata used 0 in computations (499 CE); Brahmagupta codified arithmetic properties of 0 and negative numbers (628 CE), creating what modern algebra calls a ring. The section closes with a summary diagram of the five evolutionary ideas: counting in groups, using landmark numbers, choosing powers of a base, using position for landmark numbers, and zero as digit and number.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Representing 27 in Roman numerals: 27 = 10+10+5+1+1, so XXVII.
Representing 2367 in Roman numerals by grouping into 1000s, 100s, 50s, 10s, 5s, 1s: MMCCCLXII.
Adding CCXXXII + CCCCXIII in Roman numerals: counting I's, X's, C's and regrouping (5 C's = D) to get DCXLV.
Representing 324 in Egyptian hieroglyphic numerals: 324 = 100+100+100+10+10+4, written with three 100-symbols, two 10-symbols, four 1-symbols.
Expressing 143 in the constructed base-5 system: 143 = 125+5+5+5+1+1+1, written using base-5 symbols for 125, three 5s, three 1s.
Adding two sets of Egyptian numerals: totalling 15 arc-symbols and 15 stroke-symbols, then regrouping 10 arcs into one 100-symbol and 10 strokes into one arc, to produce the final sum.
Multiplying a compound Egyptian numeral by 10 using the distributive property: (100 + 10 + 1) × 10 = 1000 + 100 + 10, each landmark number moving up one power.
Representing 640 and 7530 in the Mesopotamian system: 640 = 10×60+40 written as two positional groups; 7530 = 2×3600+5×60+30 written as three positional groups.

enumerated_exercises.description: Without using Hindu number names or numerals, devise methods for +, –, ×, ÷ using the stick-based number system (Method 1). (Math Talk)
Extend the letter-sound number system (Method 2) beyond 26 using multi-letter strings so that all numbers can be represented; propose and describe the extension scheme. (Math Talk)
Invent your own number system. (Try This)

Two inline prompts: (a) identify how Gumulgal names for 3, 4, 5, 6 are formed by counting in 2s; (b) compare the Gumulgal, Bakairi, and Bushmen systems and discuss the possible common-ancestor theory.
Represent the following numbers in the Roman system: (i) 1222, (ii) 2999, (iii) 302, (iv) 715.
Find the products of Roman landmark number pairs without converting to Hindu numerals: V×L, L×D, V×D, VII×IX.
Why does a group of indigenous people in a Pacific island use different sequences of number names to count different objects? (Math Talk)

Extend the Gumulgal system beyond 6 by counting in 2s; devise methods for +, –, ×, ÷ in that system and evaluate: (i) 9+7, (ii) 9−6, (iii) 9×4, (iv) 16÷4 — all expressed in Gumulgal names. (Math Talk)
Identify features of the Hindu number system that make it more efficient than the Roman number system. (Math Talk)
Using ideas from section 3.2, refine the number system invented in E-3. (Try This)
Represent the following numbers in the Egyptian hieroglyphic system: 10458, 1023, 2660, 784, 1111, 70707.
Decode two given Egyptian numerals (i) and (ii) and state the numbers they represent.
Write the following numbers in the constructed base-5 system using its symbols: 15, 50, 137, 293, 651.
Is there any number that cannot be represented in the base-5 system constructed in the section? Explain why or why not.
Compute the landmark numbers of a base-7 system. In general, state what the landmark numbers of a base-n system are.
Add the first given pair of Egyptian hieroglyphic numerals, regrouping as needed.
Add the second given pair of Egyptian hieroglyphic numerals, regrouping as needed.
Add the two given base-5 numerals (constructed system), remembering that 5 times a landmark number gives the next one.
Find the products of the following Egyptian landmark numbers multiplied by the 10-symbol: (i) 10×10, (ii) 100×10, (iii) 1000×10, (iv) 10000×10.
Find the products of the following Egyptian landmark numbers multiplied by the 100-symbol (10²): (i) 10×100, (ii) 100×100, (iii) 1000×100, (iv) 10000×100.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out (products, landmark pairs), p.67
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the following products of pairs of Egyptian landmark numbers: (i) 10×10000, (ii) 100×1000, (iii) 1000×1000, (iv) 10000×10000000.
Find the following products: (i) the compound numeral (99900+200+11) × 10 in Egyptian; (ii) 1000×10 in Egyptian. Identify a simple rule for multiplying by 10.
Can any Egyptian numeral have a symbol occurring 10 or more times? Explain why not. (Math Talk)
Create your own base-4 number system and represent all numbers from 1 to 16 in it. (Math Talk)
Give a simple rule for multiplying any number by 5 in the base-5 system constructed in section 3.3.II.
Represent the following numbers in the Mesopotamian (base-60 positional) system: (i) 63, (ii) 132, (iii) 200, (iv) 60, (v) 3605.
Represent the following numbers using the Mayan system: (i) 77, (ii) 100, (iii) 361, (iv) 721.
Why did the Chinese alternate Zong and Heng symbols? How would 41 look using only Zong symbols, and could it be interpreted in multiple ways? (Math Talk)
Form a base-2 place value system using 'ukasar' (=1) and 'urapon' (=0) as digits; compare this with the Gumulgal counting-by-2s system.
Where in daily life and which professions do Hindu numerals and 0 play an important role? How might life be different without them?
If humans had only 8 fingers, what base would we likely use? Write the base-10 numeral 25 in base-8, base-5, and base-2. (Math Talk)
